metrics.claim_path: no-llm/no-network (structural; the fast claim check is a local HMAC verify)

coverage.verify_error: step 'review@then/verify_branch@then/verify' failed: the LLM provider call failed: Streamed response ended without content or tool calls

findings.suggested_fix: Split compound criteria, replace subjective terms with observable outcomes, and state how each is verified.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add an acceptance criterion requiring the `mcp_registry` job to have successfully executed end-to-end on a real (or dry-run) v* tag push, with evidence that the MCP Registry reflects the updated package version (e.g., a link to the successful workflow run or a registry URL showing the new version).
findings.finding: No acceptance criterion exercises the new OIDC publishing path end-to-end in production (i.e., an actual v* tag push that triggers the job and results in a confirmed registry update). All four ACs are satisfiable by static inspection of YAML and docs content alone — the critical new code path (mcp-publisher login github-oidc → publish → live registry) could be mis-configured and all ACs would still pass green.
findings.suggested_fix: Add AC: 'A test tag push (e.g. `v0.7.1-rc.1`) triggers the `mcp_registry` job; the job completes with exit 0 and `https://registry.mcp.io/servers/io.github.navapbc/<name>` shows the updated version, confirming OIDC trust is wired correctly.' Alternatively, note explicitly that this will be verified on the next real release and document the fallback monitoring step.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Pin the mcp-publisher binary to a specific version (e.g., `MCP_PUBLISHER_VERSION=0.x.y` env var in the job) and document the upgrade path in `docs/releasing.md` so the version can be intentionally bumped.
findings.finding: The mcp-publisher binary is installed via `curl` with no version pin ("curl the release binary, os/arch-detected"), meaning any future breaking release of mcp-publisher will silently break the job with no configurable override.
findings.location: Implementation section — Steps bullet: "install mcp-publisher (curl the release binary, os/arch-detected)"
findings.suggested_fix: Add a `MCP_PUBLISHER_VERSION` env var at the job level (e.g., `env: MCP_PUBLISHER_VERSION: '0.3.1'`) and use it in the curl URL, so version bumps are a deliberate one-line change with a clear audit trail.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add explicit error handling for the `mcp-publisher` steps: specify whether the job should use `continue-on-error: true` (non-blocking), a retry action (e.g. `nick-fields/retry`), or at minimum document that a failed run must be manually re-triggered and how to detect the failure.
findings.finding: The plan specifies no retry, backoff, or error-handling strategy for the two external calls (`mcp-publisher login github-oidc` and `mcp-publisher publish`). If the MCP Registry is transiently unavailable, the job fails with no recovery path and no documented fallback other than a manual re-run.
findings.location: Implementation section — Steps: `./mcp-publisher login github-oidc` → `./mcp-publisher publish`
findings.suggested_fix: Add `continue-on-error: true` to the `mcp_registry` job (so a registry outage does not block release visibility) and add a GitHub Actions workflow failure notification (e.g. via the existing alerting channel or a `if: failure()` step that posts to Slack/issue tracker), or use a retry action with 3 attempts and 30s backoff before considering the job failed.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Confirm and document whether `mcp-publisher publish` is idempotent (i.e., returns exit 0 if the version is already present), or add a `--skip-existing`/`--if-not-exists` flag if the tool supports it, so re-runs are safe.
findings.finding: The plan does not address idempotency of `mcp-publisher publish` when the same tag workflow is re-run (e.g., manually re-triggered after an earlier partial failure). Most registries reject a second publish of the same version with a non-zero exit code, which would cause the retry itself to fail even though the state is correct.
findings.location: Implementation section — Steps; Acceptance Criteria AC1

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add an Acceptance Criterion requiring at least one successful live end-to-end execution of the `mcp_registry` job against the real MCP Registry (e.g., via a pre-release tag, a dry-run mode if supported, or a manual workflow dispatch on a test tag) before the change is considered verified.
findings.finding: The Acceptance Criteria are all satisfiable by static YAML inspection and docs review without ever exercising the new OIDC publish path end-to-end against the live MCP Registry. This is a cutover/migration that makes OIDC the DEFAULT path, yet no AC requires a successful live registry publish (the job could have a wrong OIDC audience, incorrect namespace, or bad binary invocation and all ACs would still pass green).
findings.suggested_fix: Add AC: '- [ ] The `mcp_registry` job has executed successfully end-to-end on a real `v*` tag (or a test tag if the registry supports non-production namespaces), confirming OIDC authentication and `mcp-publisher publish` complete without error.' Alternatively, if `mcp-publisher` supports a `--dry-run` flag, add an AC that a dry-run against the live registry OIDC endpoint succeeds in CI on a feature branch before merge.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Verify that `mcp-publisher login github-oidc` is the exact, current CLI subcommand for OIDC login by checking the mcp-publisher release notes or running `mcp-publisher help login` against the release binary, and add the confirmed invocation (with any required flags) to the plan.
findings.finding: The plan asserts 'mcp-publisher login github-oidc' is a valid CLI subcommand, citing 'modelcontextprotocol.io/registry/github-actions' as the source. This subcommand appears nowhere in the codebase (no hits in any file), and only the interactive 'mcp-publisher login github' is documented in docs/releasing.md. The OIDC variant's actual invocation syntax, flag names, and whether it is GA or experimental cannot be verified from any file in this repo.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Expand the `docs/releasing.md` edit scope beyond §4 to also update § 'Channels & where they live' (line 13-14) and § 'Accounts / prerequisites' (lines 27-28) to reflect that MCP Registry publishing is now OIDC-automated in CI.
findings.finding: The plan scopes `docs/releasing.md` updates to '§4' (Update the MCP Registry), but two other sections in the same file also describe the manual device-flow as the current method and will become stale: § 'Accounts / prerequisites' (line 27-28: 'MCP Registry publishing authenticates via `mcp-publisher login github`') and § 'Channels & where they live' (line 13-14: 'published with the `mcp-publisher` CLI'). These are unenumerated consumers within the target file.
findings.location: docs/releasing.md — § 'Accounts / prerequisites' (lines 27-28) and § 'Channels & where they live' (lines 13-14); Implementation section of the plan
findings.suggested_fix: In § 'Channels & where they live', update the MCP Registry bullet to: 'Published automatically by `.github/workflows/release.yml` `mcp_registry` job on a `vX.Y.Z` tag, via OIDC (`mcp-publisher login github-oidc`) — no stored secret.' In § 'Accounts / prerequisites', update or remove the bullet describing `mcp-publisher login github` as the auth method, noting that CI now handles it automatically via OIDC.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Confirm (citing the MCP Registry OIDC docs at modelcontextprotocol.io/registry/github-actions) that the `github-oidc` login path resolves the `io.github.navapbc/*` namespace from the repo's org owner rather than from a user's public org membership, and update `docs/releasing.md` §4's org-membership gotcha note to clarify whether it applies to the automated OIDC path or only to the manual fallback.
findings.finding: The plan asserts that the GitHub Actions OIDC token for navapbc/rebar automatically authorizes the io.github.navapbc/* namespace on the MCP Registry, but the existing docs/releasing.md §4 documents a gotcha that namespace claims are captured from the user's public navapbc org membership at interactive login time. These are different trust mechanisms: interactive login resolves namespace from a human user's org membership; OIDC resolves namespace from the repo's org owner. The plan does not confirm that the OIDC path handles org-namespace resolution from repo identity alone, nor does it specify that the org-membership gotcha note in §4 is superseded for the automated path. If the MCP Registry's OIDC path also requires a runtime org-membership check (e.g., the navapbc org's public-membership API at token-exchange time), a first-run 403 would block the entire release pipeline.
findings.suggested_fix: Add a sentence to the Implementation section: 'Per [registry OIDC docs], the OIDC token's `repository_owner` claim (navapbc) is used directly to authorize the `io.github.navapbc/*` namespace — no public org-membership prerequisite applies to the runner identity.' Then in docs/releasing.md §4, scope the org-membership gotcha to the manual fallback path only.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Verify that `mcp-publisher login github-oidc` is a currently-released, documented subcommand (confirm in mcp-publisher's changelog/release notes or source), and pin the minimum mcp-publisher version that introduced it in the installation step.
findings.finding: The plan asserts that `mcp-publisher login github-oidc` is a supported, shipped command, but the codebase (docs/releasing.md) only evidences `mcp-publisher login github` (browser/device flow). No version pin or mcp-publisher binary exists in the repo to confirm the `github-oidc` subcommand is real and currently available.
findings.suggested_fix: In the curl-install step, pin a specific mcp-publisher release version (e.g. `MPCPUBLISHER_VERSION=vX.Y.Z`) that is confirmed to ship `login github-oidc`, and add a `./mcp-publisher --version` step to emit evidence in CI logs.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Confirm the MCP Registry's OIDC trust model for org namespaces: document whether a one-time trust registration is required (analogous to PyPI's pending-publisher) and add it to the prerequisites in docs/releasing.md §4 and the Implementation section.
findings.finding: The plan does not resolve how OIDC-based login satisfies the MCP Registry's org-namespace authorization requirement. The existing docs (releasing.md §4) warn that publishing under `io.github.navapbc/*` requires public navapbc org membership at login time — a human-identity constraint that does not apply when the identity is a GitHub Actions runner OIDC token.
findings.location: Implementation section + docs/releasing.md §4 ('Update the MCP Registry')

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Verify that `mcp-publisher login github-oidc` is a shipped subcommand by checking the mcp-publisher release binary's `--help` output or changelog, and update the plan with the confirmed exact invocation (or pivot to the correct subcommand name/flags if it differs).
findings.finding: The plan's central CLI subcommand `mcp-publisher login github-oidc` is UNVERIFIED: it appears nowhere in the codebase (only `mcp-publisher login github` device-flow is documented and evidenced), and the cited reference URL `modelcontextprotocol.io/registry/github-actions` is absent from the repo. If this subcommand does not exist or has a different name/flag, the entire job fails at the login step.
findings.location: Implementation section: `./mcp-publisher login github-oidc` step; also AC line 1
findings.suggested_fix: Before implementing, run `./mcp-publisher login --help` against the downloaded binary and confirm the `github-oidc` argument exists. If the feature is not yet shipped, add a SPIKE task to validate it against a pre-release or track the upstream issue, and hold the plan until confirmed.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Confirm and document the MCP Registry's OIDC authorization preconditions for the `io.github.navapbc/*` namespace (e.g., any one-time registry-side trust configuration analogous to PyPI's pending-publisher setup), and add them to the plan's prerequisites section.
findings.finding: The plan does not address how the MCP Registry's OIDC trust policy authorizes the machine identity to publish under the `io.github.navapbc/*` org namespace. The existing docs describe an org-membership check that is captured at device-flow login time; whether OIDC replaces or bypasses this check (and what the registry requires — e.g., the repo must be in the navapbc org, navapbc membership must be public in GitHub's OIDC claims, etc.) is asserted but not specified.
findings.location: Implementation section: auth/OIDC preconditions; docs/releasing.md §4 'Gotcha' note

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Pin `mcp-publisher` to a specific release version in the `curl` install step AND verify the downloaded binary's SHA-256 checksum (or GPG signature) before executing it, so a future CDN compromise or unintended breaking release cannot run untrusted code with the `id-token: write` OIDC credential in scope.
findings.finding: The `mcp-publisher` binary is downloaded via `curl` with no version pin and no integrity verification (no checksum / signature check), then executed on the same runner that holds the `id-token: write` OIDC token. A compromised CDN, MITM, or future malicious release could serve a tampered binary that exfiltrates the OIDC token or publishes a poisoned `server.json` to the MCP Registry.
findings.location: Implementation — Steps: 'install mcp-publisher (curl the release binary, os/arch-detected)'
findings.suggested_fix:
In the install step, pin to a specific version tag (e.g. `v0.3.0`) and verify the checksum:
```yaml
- name: Install mcp-publisher
  env:
    MCP_PUB_VERSION: "0.3.0"
    MCP_PUB_SHA256: "<sha256-of-linux-amd64-binary>"
  run: |
    curl -fsSL -o mcp-publisher \
      "https://github.com/modelcontextprotocol/mcp-publisher/releases/download/v${MCP_PUB_VERSION}/mcp-publisher-linux-amd64"
    echo "${MCP_PUB_SHA256}  mcp-publisher" | sha256sum -c
    chmod +x mcp-publisher
```
Update `MCP_PUB_VERSION` and `MCP_PUB_SHA256` in the same commit that bumps to a new mcp-publisher release.
